content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : File                                    Date : 4-29-77


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Ted Prim

Subject: Time test for becoming a lobbyist pursuant to 18239.

A person can become a lobbyist in three ways: One, spending
forty hours ten of which are in direct communication; two,
spending 200 hours in direct testimony before an agency;
three, spending 40 hours in direct testimony and one hour
in direct communication other than testimony.

The comment to Section 18239 provides that time spent by
a person preparing research shall not be counted in determining whether that person is a lobbyist unless the research is
provided directly and immediately to the agency.

Direct testimony includes preparation time for written submissions and testimony which will be a part of the agency's
record. However, a provision which modifies subsection (e)
of 18239 states that research time shall not be considered
for the purpose of determining whether a person is a lobbyist pursuant to that sub-part. Accordingly, background research would appear to be excluded by an arbitrary line which
separates "research" from "time spent preparing written submissions or testimony."


TP:jo

sections.question_synthetic: What are the different ways a person can become a lobbyist under section 18239, and how is research time counted toward the threshold for becoming a lobbyist?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A person can become a lobbyist through three pathways: (1) spending 40 hours with 10 in direct communication, (2) spending 200 hours in direct testimony before an agency, or (3) spending 40 hours in direct testimony and 1 hour in direct communication. Research time is generally excluded unless provided directly and immediately to an agency, though a distinction exists between background research and time spent preparing written submissions or testimony.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is internal FPPC memorandum without formal Q/C sections. Some text is degraded/unclear in original document. No formal conclusion section but substantive guidance provided. Extracted content from body of memo treating it as FPPC advice on statutory interpretation.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the different ways a person can become a lobbyist under section 18239, and how is research time counted toward the threshold for becoming a lobbyist?

CONCLUSION: A person can become a lobbyist through three pathways: (1) spending 40 hours with 10 in direct communication, (2) spending 200 hours in direct testimony before an agency, or (3) spending 40 hours in direct testimony and 1 hour in direct communication. Research time is generally excluded unless provided directly and immediately to an agency, though a distinction exists between background research and time spent preparing written submissions or testimony.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Memorandum To : File Date : 4-29-77 From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Ted Prim Subject: Time test for becoming a lobbyist pursuant to 18239. A person can become a lobbyist in three ways: One, spending forty hours ten of which are in direct communication; two, spending 200 hours in direct testimony before an agency; three, spending 40 hours in direct testimony and one hour in direct communication other than testimony. The comment to Section 18239 provides that time spent by a person preparing research shall not be counted in determining whether that person is a lobbyist unless the research is provided directly and immediately to the agency. Direct testimony includes preparation time for written submissions and testimony which will be a part of the agency's record. However, a provision which modifies subsection (e) of 18239 states that research time shall not be considered for the purpose of determining whether a person is a lobbyist pursuant to that sub-part. Accordingly, background research would appear to be excluded by an arbitrary line which separates "research" from "time spent preparing written submissions or testimony." TP:jo
embedding.summary: FPPC memorandum explaining the three methods by which a person becomes a lobbyist under section 18239 and clarifying how research time is counted toward meeting the threshold, noting that background research is excluded unless directly provided to an agency.
